content.full_text:
June 5, 1975

75-06-142

Mr. James Self, Councilman
City of San Jose
801 North First Street
San Jose, California 95110

Dear Mr. Self:

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-124

Thank you for your letter of May 13, 1975 requesting an
opinion with respect to your duties under the Political Reform
Act.

Since your request raises no substantial question involving
interpretation of the Political Reform Act, your request for a
formal opinion is denied.

The determination of the fair market value of the wood with
which you deal is a question of fact, not one of law. Although
the determination of fair market value may be difficult, a
reasonable and good faith estimation on your part would be
acceptable. Further questions on valuation of the wood should
be addressed to your city attorney.

Assuming that your dealings with the owner are at arm's
length, it would be presumed that equal value was provided on
both sides and the receipt of wood from owners of barns would
not constitute a gift to you. This would be true whether the
owner paid you or you paid the owner, or no money changed hands.
If, on the other hand, you were not dealing at arm's length,
and the owner was for some reason doing you a favor by letting
you have the wood, a gift might be involved. Only in such an
event would the person providing you with the wood be a source
of income.

The question of whether your business would be materially
affected by a matter before the city council is also one which
would turn on the specific facts. You should consult with your
city attorney when such questions arise. On the other hand, the
criteria set forth in your letter seem quite reasonable.

Mr. James Self
June 5, 1975
Page Two

Although for the reason stated no formal opinion will be
issued, I hope the foregoing is sufficiently helpful. If you
have any further questions, I urge you to contact a member of
the Commission staff.

Sincerely,

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, for the
Commission

DHL:cjb

CITY OF SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA

801 NORTH FIRST STREET
SAN JOSE, CA 95110
(408) 277-4200

JAMES SELF
COUNCILMAN

May 13, 1975

Fair Political Practices Commission
925 "J" Street, Suite 522
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Opinion request pursuant to
Government Code §83114

Dear Commissioners:

I am a member of the San Jose City Council, and I am writing to request
an opinion of the Commission pursuant to Gov. Code §83114 with respect
to my duties under Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act of 1974.

The material facts which give rise to my need for an opinion are as
follows. Members of our City Council are only paid $400 per month,
which necessitates outside sources of income. In my case, I have
recently begun a business with two other people which involves the
demolition of old barns and the sale of the old barn wood from these
barns. The business is a general partnership and it is called Siltoo.
We have been in business for approximately three months.

The standard size of the boards (old barn wood) is 1" x 12".
Prices
for this wood fluctuate from 35¢ to $3 per foot, depending on the type
of demand (to whom we're selling - furniture manufacturer or interior
decorator), the quality and the quantity. There is no set market
value for old barn wood, and therefore the price is unpredictable.
This is particularly relevant when I attempt to value wood we receive
in certain transactions in terms of gifts or income, since we do not
always have a ready customer at a set price when we receive the wood.

Many of these old barns are located upon real property owned by developers or other persons who have permission to more barns the City
Council for zoning action or subdivision appraisal.

Arrangements for our partnership to acquire old barn wood may
occur in three ways: 1) the property owner may give the barn to us
at no charge, with the understanding that we remove the barn down to
the raw ground; 2) we may pay a fee to the owner for the wood - this
fee usually is anywhere from $50 to $300; and, 3) the owner may pay us
for the demolition of the building and we would also receive all the
wood. Under this situation we are responsible for the complete demolition of the building and the disposal of all materials from that demolition, including those we do not choose to keep. The process of
removing a barn has a number of overhead costs to us. Our labor cost

Request No. 75 124
Received.S-2-15-75
Date Due 6-10-75

Fair Political Practices Commission
May 13, 1975
Page 2

is approximately $3.00 per hour. We own a 13-foot flatbed truck, an
estimated value of $1,500, annual insurance premium of $350 on that
vehicle, and an investment in various types of tools estimated at $-50
presently, plus warehouse fees of approximately $300 per month for storage.
We also maintain a large cash outlay of an undermined amount at this
time for workmen's liability insurance.

I am concerned about the application of Articles 1 and 2 of Chapter 7
of the Political Reform Act of 1974 to the three situations I outlined
above. The difficulty in assessing these situations stems, in part,
from how to value the old barn wood which our business generates in the
possible types of transactions I have enumerated above. If we
perform the demolition of the barn and receive the wood in exchange,
or where we purchase the wood, how do we value the wood to determine
whether the wood or any part of it constitutes a gift? As you know,
that term is defined in Gov. Code §32253 as "any payment to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received." This
valuation poses substantial problems for me, because at the time we
acquire the wood, there may be no definite customer for it and consequently the potential price we will receive may fluctuate dramatically
from $50 to $3 per foot. Yet, I must take some valuation at the time
we receive it in order to determine if I have received a gift for the
purposes of Gov. Code §37200 or §37205. I assume that it there is a
specific customer at a fixed price, then in terms of that transaction
the value is the price at which we actually sell the wood.

Another question which requires your opinion is whether in either of
these situations - where we remove the barn and receive the wood or
where we pay for the wood - the person from whom we receive the wood
is to be treated as a source of income for purposes of Chapter 7.
The question of how to value the barn wood is also important in determining the value of the "income" we receive when we are paid with the
barn wood, if you conclude that the person who pays us the wood is a
source of my income.

Please provide me with an opinion regarding my duties and responsibilities under Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act with regard to
the three types of transactions in which our partnership receives old
barn wood, outlined in the fifth paragraph of this letter. As I noted
above, I am particularly concerned about the proper manner of valuing
this wood, whether the transaction falls in the "gift" category or
the "source of income" category.

The answers to the foregoing questions are essential to me in determining how to report certain transactions on my disclosure statement,
which must be filed on or before July 1, 1975, pursuant to Gov. Code
§27528, and in determining what actions or decisions I must refrain
from participating in under §37109.

Fair Political Practices Commission
May 15, 1975
Page 3

We also request your consideration of another related aspect of these
questions. Since I have an investment in Sitco which is worth more than
$1,000 (Gov. Code §87103(a)) and since I am also a partner of that
business entity (Gov. Code §87103(d)), would these interests prohibit
me from voting or participating in voting, subdivision or other actions
affecting real property which has an old barn located on it under Gov.
Code §87100? I assume that I would be prohibited from participating
in any City Council action affecting such real property if Sitco has agreements, arrangements or commitments relative to a particular
barn with the owner of the real property in question. However, if there
were no such prior agreements, would you conclude that my interests in
Sitco would be materially affected financially by city Council actions
such as zoning or subdivision approval, which could have the potential
of facilitating the development of the real property in question and
perhaps thereby making the barn wood from the barn on that property
available. I think that Sitco may be the only business concern in this
area which is engaged in this business, but I cannot be certain on
this point, and there are other individuals who purchase old barn wood
from time to time.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this matter. I am hopeful that
the Commission will provide me with an opinion on these questions which
will remove my uncertainty about my duties under the Political Reform
Act of 1974. If I can provide the Commission with any additional information on this matter, please do not hesitate to contact me at (403)
277-4000, extension 4241.

Very truly yours,

JAMES SELF,
Councilman
